Let's take a look at a study that illustrates the impact of a second amplifier, which is the public nature of a commitment. This research was done at the University of North Carolina with students who were on academic probation because they were having a hard time keeping their grades up. If they couldn't raise their grades by a full letter before the end of the semester, these students would be dismissed from the University. So the stakes were high for them and for their academic advisors, whose job it was to help them succeed. The researchers randomly split the students into three experimental conditions. The first group was not asked to set any study goal. The second group was asked to set a study goal related to improving their grades, but were asked to keep that goal private. The third group was asked to set a study goal, write the goal down, sign their names, and make copies to share with the entire class. Now, keep in mind that the instructional material and assistance from advisors were exactly the same for all three groups. The results were that in the no goal condition only 14% of students actually raised their grades enough to stay in school. And in the private goal condition, these students had exactly the same result, only 14% succeeded. But in the last condition, where the students made their goal public, 86% of them raised their grade by a full letter. So what's the implication for you? After gaining a preliminary agreement from a customer or client, ask them to share the good news within their organization, and let them know that you've done the same within your organization by sending them a message saying something like, I was so happy about your intention today to move forward on a full agreement by next week that I told everyone on my staff who were thrilled to know that we'll be working with you on this. You will have significantly increased the likelihood that the preliminary agreement will be fulfilled.
